coredata.dc:title: Room temperature phosphorescent carbon dots for latent fingerprints detection and in vivo phosphorescence bioimaging
coredata.dc:creator: Feng, Qi
Xie, Zhigang
Zheng, Min
coredata.dc:description: Up to date, most of room temperature phosphorescent (RTP) carbon dots (CDs) have been applied to information encryption, anticounterfeiting and sensing. However, quite a few studies on RTP CDs for latent fingerprints (LFPs) detection and in vivo bioimaging. In the present work, CDs-based luminophore (CD71) was prepared from neutral red, KNO3, MgCl2 and KH2PO4 and it maintains high phosphorescence quantum yield (24.4% and 22.5%) and long lifetime (546 ms and 616 ms), whether in solid or solution state, which is in the high level for CDs-based RTP phosphors heretofore. Benefit from the unique RTP performance of CD71, CD71 powder is successfully utilized as an efficient phosphorescence marker for LPFs detection with high contrast and wide applicability. While CD71 solution is a prominent phosphorescence probe for in vivo afterglow bioimaging.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Room temperature phosphorescent (RTP)
Carbon dots (CDs)
Latent fingerprints (LFPs)
In vivo afterglow bioimaging
Phosphorescence probe

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Effect of mass ratios of MgCl2/KH2PO4 (from 3:1–8:1) on the phosphorescence intensity of CDs composites.

(a) The UV–vis absorption spectrum of CD71 powder. (b) The fluorescence excitation spectrum (black solid line) monitored at 416 nm and the emission spectra of CD71 powder under different excitation wavelength. (c) The afterglow excitation spectrum of CD71 monitored at 520 nm (black solid line) and phosphorescence emission spectra of CD71 powder under different excitation wavelength. (d) The afterglow decay curve of CD71 powder under the excitation of 340 nm, (f) Digital photos of fluorescence and phosphorescence CD71 powder.

(a) The UV–vis absorption spectrum of CD71 solution. (b) The fluorescence excitation spectrum of CD71 aqueous solution (black solid line) and the emission spectra of CD71 aqueous solution. (c) The afterglow excitation spectrum monitored at 520 nm and phosphorescence emission spectra of CD71 aqueous solution. (d) The afterglow decay curve of CD71 aqueous solution. (e) Digital photos of fluorescence and phosphorescence of CD71 solution.
(a) TEM image of CD71. (b) FT-IR and XPS spectra of CD71. (d) XRD pattern of CD71.

(a) Fluorescence and phosphorescence photographs of LFPs on tin foil and the image of fingerprint aged for 15 days. (b) Photograsph of LFPs on glass slide and the specific details of the fingerprints. (c) Photographs of LFPs on copper plat.
The phosphorescence imaging of the mouse with the subcutaneous injection of CD71.
Schematic illustration for the synthesis and applications of CD71 in LFPs detection and in vivo afterglow imaging.

head.ce:title: Room temperature phosphorescent carbon dots for latent fingerprints detection and in vivo phosphorescence bioimaging

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Life Science, Advanced Institute of Materials Science, Changchun University of Technology, 2055 Yan'an Street, Changchun, Jilin 130022, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Life Science, Advanced Institute of Materials Science, Changchun University of Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Life Science, Advanced Institute of Materials Science, Changchun University of Technology, 2055 Yan an Street, Changchun, Jilin 130022, P. R. China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Polymer Physics and Chemistry, Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences, 5625 Renmin Street, Changchun, Jilin 130022, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Polymer Physics and Chemistry, Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Polymer Physics and Chemistry, Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences, 5625 Renmin Street, Changchun, Jilin 130022, P. R. China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Up to date, most of room temperature phosphorescent (RTP) carbon dots (CDs) have been applied to information encryption, anticounterfeiting and sensing. However, quite a few studies on RTP CDs for latent fingerprints (LFPs) detection and in vivo bioimaging. In the present work, CDs-based luminophore (CD71) was prepared from neutral red, KNO3, MgCl2 and KH2PO4 and it maintains high phosphorescence quantum yield (24.4% and 22.5%) and long lifetime (546 ms and 616 ms), whether in solid or solution state, which is in the high level for CDs-based RTP phosphors heretofore. Benefit from the unique RTP performance of CD71, CD71 powder is successfully utilized as an efficient phosphorescence marker for LPFs detection with high contrast and wide applicability. While CD71 solution is a prominent phosphorescence probe for in vivo afterglow bioimaging.

ce:section.ce:para: Fingerprints have great significance in the research, evaluation and identification of evidence at the scene of serious crimes, due to their uniqueness and permanence. In forensic identification, the identity characteristics of criminals can be accurately determined by identifying the fingerprint ridges of fingerprints. However, most of the fingerprints form latent fingerprints (LFPs) and are hard to be identified with the naked eyes, because of the aging and deposition of fingerprints. Until now, substantial of methods have been developed for the detection and visualization of LFPs. Among them, fluorescence imaging that dusting fluorescent powders on LFPs to obtain high-contrast fingerprint images under the irradiation of light, is regarded as an efficient approach to be widely used in detecting LFPs on various surfaces [1–7]. Nevertheless, some disadvantages including low detection sensitivity, poor contrast, high background interference, complicated production process, and potential toxicity seriously limit its practical applications. Therefore, it is urgent to explore a more convenient and efficient imaging technique to address the aforementioned drawbacks. Solid room temperature phosphorescence (RTP) materials can protect the details of LFPs and illuminate LFPs with higher contrast and lower background interference, which are considered as an effective strategy to enhance the visualization of LFPs.

On the other hand, optical imaging techniques such as fluorescence [8–10] and bioluminescence [11–13] imaging play important roles in biomedical fields. Up to now, many fluorescent agents have been utilized as probes for in vivo imaging. However, intense tissue autofluorescence induced by the external excitation light usually diminishes the signal-to-noise ratio (SNR). Bioluminescence takes advantage of the specific interactions of enzymes and substrates to generate optical signals without the aid of extra light excitation, thus it can circumvent the nonspecific fluorescence interference. Nevertheless, the defects of high cost, complex experimental procedures and limited luciferase hindered their potential applications. Phosphorescence imaging still maintains luminescent after ceasing the excitation light, which can efficiently eliminate autofluorescence interference. Compared with fluorescence and bioluminescence imaging, phosphorescence imaging is advantageous in accordance with higher SNR and better sensitivity. In order to obtain high-quality phosphorescence images of biological tissue, exploiting RTP materials with long phosphorescence lifetime, high phosphorescence quantum yield (PQY) and good water dispersibility is a promising strategy.

As a new type of optical nanomaterials, carbon dots (CDs) have wide potential applications in sensing [14–17], bioimaging [18–20], cancer therapy [21–24], optoelectronic devices [25–27], and catalyst, owe to their unique optical properties, high water dispersibility, excellent photostability, and good biocompatibility. So far, multitudinous fluorescent CDs were reported, by contrast, the research on RTP CDs is relatively uncommon. Moreover, CDs-based RTP materials are mostly focused on the solid state, because phosphorescence can be easily quenched by water and dissolved oxygen, impeding their practical application in bioimaging. For example, our group prepared boron-doped carbon dots (BDs) with high PQY (34.4%) and long RTP lifetime (2.61 s). Unfortunately, no afterglow was presented in BDs aqueous solution [28]. Up to date, some RTP CDs have been applied to information encryption [29–33], anticounterfeiting [30,34–39] and sensing [40,41]. However, few LFPs detection and in vivo bioimaging applications of RTP CDs have been exploited yet [42–46].

In the present work, CDs-based luminophores were successfully synthesized using neutral red, KNO3, MgCl2 and KH2PO4 as precursors via molten salt method. By varying the mass ratio of MgCl2 to KH2PO4, a series of phosphors were obtained. When the mass ratio was 7:1, the phosphor have the strongest phosphorescence and was named as CD71 ( Scheme 1). In the solid state, CD71 presented intense yellow RTP, which was visible to the naked eyes for 7 s. The PQY and phosphorescence lifetime is 24.4% and 546 ms, respectively. In the aqueous solution, CD71 still emitted yellow afterglow and lasted for 8 s, with PQY and afterglow lifetime of 22.5% and 616 ms, respectively. Whether in solid or solution state, CD71 remains high PQY and long lifetime, which are in the high level for CDs-based RTP phosphors heretofore. Motivated by the excellent luminous performance, solid CD71 was utilized as a stable dusting material for effective LFPs detection and the CD71 solution served as an effective probe for in vivo phosphorescence imaging.

In order to optimize the reaction condition, the mass of neutral red (0.025 g), KNO3 (1.0 g) and KH2PO4 (0.05 g) was kept unchanged, while the mass of MgCl2 was increased from 0.15 to 0.2, 0.25, 0.3, 0.35 and 0.4 g, respectively. Correspondingly, the mass ratio of MgCl2/KH2PO4 was 3:1, 4:1, 5:1, 6:1, 7:1 and 8:1 and the sample was named as CD31, CD41, CD51, CD61, CD71 and CD81, respectively. Taking CD71 as an example to make a brief introduction: Neutral red (0.025 g), KNO3 (1.0 g), KH2PO4 (0.05 g) and MgCl2 (0.35 g) were dissolved in 10 mL of ultrapure water and stirred for 10 min to form transparent solution. The mixture was freeze-dried and then added into the crucible, and heated to 360 °C for 1.5 h at a rate of 10 °C min−1 in the muffle furnace. Finally, CD71 was obtained and stored for further applications. In the reaction, neutral red was carbon source, which was fully carbonized to form CDs, KNO3 acted as a salt medium to accelerate the progress of MgCl2 and KH2PO4 translating into the liquid state and confined CDs in the molten salt. After cooled to room temperature, the recrystallized MgCl2 and KH2PO4 formed an insoluble salt shell, which successfully achieved RTP in aqueous solution. In this system, the mass ratio of MgCl2 and KH2PO4 is crucial for the phosphorescence performance of CDs. As shown in Fig. 1, all the samples in solid or aqueous forms emit blue fluorescence under the irradiation of a 365 nm UV lamp, while produce yellow RTP after ceasing the UV lamp. When the mass ratio of MgCl2/KH2PO4 is 3:1, CD31 showed weak phosphorescence. As the mass ratio increased, the phosphorescence intensity of CDs gradually enhanced and reached the strongest when the mass ratio was 7:1 (Fig. S1). Thus, CD71 was regarded as the optimal product for the following characterization and applications.

The UV-Vis absorption spectrum of solid CD71 ( Fig. 2a) powder displays two typical absorption peaks at 302 nm and 354 nm, which can be attributed to the π → π * transition of CC and n → π * transition of CO, respectively. As depicted in Fig. 2b and c, the fluorescence and afterglow excitation spectra overlap with the absorption bands at 300–400 nm, demonstrating that both the fluorescence and phosphorescence mainly come from the CO in CD71. The emission spectrum of CD71 powder displays the typical excitation dependent behavior (Fig. 2b). The emission peak mainly locates at 405 nm and gradually red shifts to 490 nm, with the excitation wavelength increasing from 320 to 420 nm. The optimal emission of CD71 powder is located at 416 nm under 360 nm of excitation. By contrast, the phosphorescence spectrum demonstrates that CD71 powder does not exhibit obvious excitation wavelength dependence behavior, and the optimal phosphorescence emission is observed at 518 nm under 340 nm of excitation (Fig. 2c). Fig. 2e depicts that CD71 presents blue fluorescence under the excitation of 365 nm, whereas emits yellow afterglow after removing the UV lamp. The bright yellow RTP can be effortlessly observed by the naked eyes and lasted for 7 s. The afterglow decay spectrum of CD71 powder at 340 nm excitation can be fitted to a triexponential functions with lifetime components to be 27.22 (11.6%), 166.5 (45.35%) and 653.2 (43.04) ms (Fig. 2d), suggesting the existence of multiple excited triplet states of CD71 powder. The average lifetime of CD71 powder is calculated to be 546 ms, which is relatively longer than other CDs-based RTP materials [47,48]. The absolute phosphorescence quantum yield (PQY) of CD71 powder is about 24.4%, which is substantially high for non metallic RTP materials.

It is well known that RTP materials usually undergo extensive quenching in the presence of water. The as-prepared aqueous solution of CD71 exhibits strong yellow phosphorescence. To date, CDs-based RTP phenomenon in aqueous solution is rarely reported. CD71 aqueous solution exhibits blue fluorescence under the excitation of 365 nm, after turning off the UV lamp, the yellow afterglow of CD71 can be observed with the naked eyes for up to 8 s ( Fig. 3e). The UV−vis absorption spectrum of CD71 aqueous solution is shown in Fig. 3a, it has two typical absorption peaks centered at 306 nm and 355 nm, respectively. The maximum fluorescence of CD71 aqueous solution is at 412 nm under the excitation of 360 nm (Fig. 3b). The optimal afterglow emission of CD71 aqueous solution is at 520 nm when 340 nm of excitation was ceased (Fig. 3c). The phosphorescence decay curve of CD71 aqueous solution can be fitted by the triexponential functions with three lifetime components: 28 ms (12.98%), 189 ms (46.25%) and 746 ms (40.77%), the average afterglow lifetime was deduced to be 616 ms (Fig. 3d and Table S1). The PQY of CD71 in aqueous environment was measured to be 22.5%. Based on the results mentioned above, CD71 aqueous solution possesses the similar optical properties with CD71 solid.

The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image of CD71 ( Fig. 4a) illustrates that CDs are monodispersed in the salt matrix. The surface groups and chemical compositions of CD71 were characterized by Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra. There are characteristic bands at 3385 (-NH/-OH), 2893 (PO4 3−/HPO4 2−/H2PO4 −), 1754 (CO), 1635 (CC), 1366 (C-O/C-N/NO3 -), 1045 (PO4 3−/HPO4 2−/H2PO4 −) and 820 (CC) cm−1 in the FTIR spectrum (Fig. 4b). In the XPS spectra, the peaks at 532.0 (O 1s), 406.5 (N 1 s), 376.9 (K 2 s), 352.1 (Mg KLL), 327.3 (Mg KLL), 306.4 (Mg KLL), 292.5 (K 2p), 284.2 (C 1 s), 268.8 (Cl 2 s), 197.7 (Cl 2p), 132.2 (P 2p), 88.3 (Mg 2 s), 50.0 (Mg 2p), 32.6 (K 3 s) and 15.2 eV (Cl 3 s) verified the existence of C, N, O, Mg, K, P and Cl elements (Fig. 4c and Fig. S2). The C 1s XPS spectrum (Fig. S2a) contains three components that can be deconvoluted into C−C/CC (284.5 eV), C−N/C−P (292.8) and O−CO (295.6 eV), respectively. The N 1 s XPS spectrum (Fig. S2b) displays two components at 403.6 eV and 407.2 eV, which can be assigned to C−N/N − P and NO3 −. The O 1s XPS spectrum (Fig. S2c) indicates the existence of C−O−C (531.3 eV), PO (532.8 eV) and CO (533.9 eV) in CD71. The Cl 2p XPS spectrum (Fig. S2d) can be fitted to a peak centered at 198.8 eV, which belongs to Cl-. The P 2p XPS spectrum (Fig. S2e) shows two peaks at 131.6 eV and 133.6 eV, which belong to PC/P − N and PO, respectively. In the Mg 2p XPS spectrum (Fig. S2f), the signal at 50.92 eV reveals the appearance of Mg-C. X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern of CD71 solid is shown in Fig. 4d, the peak at 19.5° is attributed to the (002) plane of graphite, indicating CDs are in the crystalline state. In addition, the peaks at 23.8°, 29.6° and 41.3° correspond to the lattice facets of MgHPO4.3 H2O, Mg3(PO4)2 and MgO, respectively. Based on the above analysis, we speculated the possible mechanism for CD71 to produce RTP. First of all, the introduction of Mg ions can effectively promote the intersystem crossing (ISC) process between the singlet state and the triplet state. The ∆EST was calculated to be 0.49 eV, which is an efficient value for ISC. In addition, the formed insoluble salt matrix can protect excited triplet species and suppress non-radiative transitions. The massive aggregation of the salt matrix further improves the rigidity of the structure and prevents water molecules from quenching the phosphorescence of CD71.

Thanks to the unique RTP performance of CD71, it is a promising indicator for LFPs detection. Herein, powder dusting technique that utilizes the mechanical adhesion of powder to the oily components of fingerprint friction ridge deposits was used for detecting LFPs. CD71 powder can effectively adhere on the fingerprint residues of diverse substrates including aluminum foil ( Fig. 5a), glass (Fig. 5b) and copper plates (Fig. 5c) by powder spraying. As seen in Fig. 5a under the irradiation of 365 nm UV light, the clarity of fingerprints was improved. However, the contrast was low due to the interference of background fluorescence. As the UV light was turned off, cleancut ridges and edges were observed without background staining, indicating CD71 is an ideal phosphorescent imaging agent for fingerprint identification. Moreover, the resolution and sensitivity decayed slightly even after 15 days of fingerprint deposition, indicating that CD71 powder has a low detection limit in aged fingerprint forensics. Similarly, when applying CD71 powder on glass, LFPs were blurred and it was difficult to obtain a distinct vision of an integral fingerprint pattern under natural light (Fig. 5b). Upon the excitation of 365 nm, a relatively complete fingerprint was found with autofluorescence background. After ceasing the UV lamp, explicit and holonomic bright yellow fingerprint presented. Moreover, the fingerprint image with higher magnification are shown on the right side of Fig. 5b, fingerprint details such as bifurcation, whorl and termination can be clearly identified, which are of great significance in forensic identification. When the fingerprint on copper plates was stained by CD71 powder, the clear fingerprint image also appeared (Fig. 5c). The above results demonstrate that CD71 powder has wide prospects in the application of LPFs imaging with high contrast and wide applicability.

The preeminent RTP performance of CD71 aqueous solution prompts us to study its feasibility in in vivo afterglow imaging. The well-dispersed CD71 solution was injected into the hind leg area of mice by subcutaneous injection. Under the irradiation of UV light, the fur of mouse emitted strong autofluorescence and interrupted the fluorescence signals of CD71. After removing the UV lamp, the intense afterglow signal in the leg area was clearly visible due to the elimination of tissue autofluorescence ( Fig. 6). Moreover, the signal could be visible to the naked eyes for 4 s. This result highlights the unique advantage of using CD71 luminogen for afterglow imaging.

In summary, we successfully synthesized CD71 luminogen by using neutral red, KNO3, MgCl2 and KH2PO4 as starting materials. The introduction of Mg metal ions can accelerate the ISC rate, and the insoluble salt shell suppresses the non-radiative transition and prevents the quenching of phosphorescence by water molecules. The aggregation of salt shell further improves the structural rigidity and prolongs the phosphorescence lifetime. Due to the excellent RTP performance of CD71 powder and aqueous solution, CD71 powder could successfully act as an effective phosphorescence marker to visualize LPFs with high contrast. On the other hand, CD71 solution is a promising afterglow probe for in vivo bioimaging. This work provides a novel strategy to develop a unique phosphor for LFPs detection and in vivo afterglow imaging.

Qi Feng: Methodology, Investigation, Software, Writing – original draft. Zhigang Xie: Resources, Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Min Zheng: Conceptualization, Resources, Supervision, Writing – review & editing, Data curation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 51873023), the Jilin Province Science and Technology Research Project (No. 20200201088JC).

ce:section.ce:para: All animal experiments comply with the ARRIVE guidelines and the British Animal (Scientific Procedures) Act 1986 and related guidelines.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.snb.2021.130976.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Red fluorescent carbon dot powder for accurate latent fingerprint identification using an artificial intelligence program
ACS Appl. Mater. Interfaces

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X.Y. Dong, X.Q. Niu, Z.Y. Zhang, J.S. Wei, H.M. Xiong, Red fluorescent carbon dot powder for accurate latent fingerprint identification using an artificial intelligence program, ACS Appl. Mater. Interfaces. 12 (2020) 29549–29555.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Qi Feng is currently a master student in the School of Chemistry and Life Sciences of Changchun University of Technology. He obtained his BS degree from Changchun University of Technology (2017). His research interests focuses on phosphorescent materials.

Zhigang Xie is a full professor at Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry (CIAC), Chinese Academy of Science (CAS). Dr Xie obtained his BS degree in Chemistry from Henan University in 2002 and his PhD degree in Polymer Chemistry from CIAC in 2007. Before joining CIAC in 2012, he worked as a postdoctoral fellow at the Polytechnic University at Brooklyn and the University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill. His research focuses on polymeric nanomedicines and fluorescent nanoparticles. Group Web: http://pnm.ciac.jl.cn/.

Min Zheng obtained her B.S. degree from Henan University in 2002 and Ph.D. from Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences in 2007. After working as a postdoctoral fellow at the University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill, she joined Changchun Institute of Optics, Fine Mechanics, and Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences. She moved to Changchun University of Technology in 2015 as a full professor. Her research interests focus on developing fluorescent nanomaterials for biological applications.
